nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Lee
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sang, Jae, Jong, Jung, Sung, Yong, Kyung, Seung, Dong, Kwang, Myung.

English: topographic name for someone who lived near a meadow or a patch of arable land, Middle English lee, lea, from Old English lēa, dative case (used after a preposition) of lēah, which originally meant ‘wood or glade’.

English: habitational name from any of the many places in England named with Old English lēah ‘wood, glade’, including Lee in Buckinghamshire, Essex, Hampshire, Kent, and Shropshire, and Lea in Cheshire, Derbyshire, Herefordshire, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, and Wiltshire.

variants.name: Ley
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
German: from a medieval personal name, Eloy, from Latin Eligius (see Eligio ), a Christian saint's name.
German (Rhineland): topographic name from Middle High German leie ‘rock, stone, slate’, or a habitational name from any of several places called with this word. Compare Leier .

variants.name: Lees
meanings: English and Scottish: topographic name from Middle English lees ‘fields, arable land’, plural of lee (see Lee ), or from Middle English lese ‘pasture, meadow’ (Old English lǣs).

English: habitational name fromLeece or Lees in Lancashire, or Leese in Cheshire, all named from OldEnglish lēas ‘woodland clearings’ (plural oflēah), or from Leece in Cumbria, which was probably namedwith a Celtic word, lïss ‘hall, court, the principalhouse in a district’.
English: variant of   Leece   1.

variants.name: Lay
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Scottish: shortened form of   McClay  .
German: habitational name from any of the places so named, in the Rhineland near Koblenz and in Bavaria, from lay(h), a word meaning ‘stone, rock, slate’.

variants.name: Laye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

variants.name: Lie
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Oystein, Sven, Thor.  German Hans, Gunther, Hilde.  Korean Han, Chang Ho, Chen, Chun, Hok, Jung, Kam, Liang, Lie, Ling, Shuen, Yung.
Norwegian: variant of Li 11, a habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads named Li, from Old Norse hlíth ‘mountain slope, hillside’. In Norway this is the most frequent spelling.
German: unexplained.

variants.name: Layman
meanings: English: topographic name for a dweller by a woodland clearing or a meadow, from Middle English ley + man. Compare Lee .
English: nickname from layman, a word denoting an uneducated person.
Americanized form of German   Lehmann  .

variants.name: Lea
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surnames 李 and 利, see   Li   1 and 3.

variants.name: Lye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee   1, a topographic name for someone who lived near a patch of arable land, or a habitational name from Lye in Herefordshire (with the same etymology).

Norwegian: habitational name from a farmstead in Rogaland named Lye, Old Norse Lýgi meaning ‘alliance, covenant’, used to denote a place sanctified by such an agreement, such as a court or council meeting place.
Altered form of German   Ley  .

variants.name: Saelee
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Chan, Kao, Nai, Chai, Cheng, Chien, Foo, Hin, Muang, Pao, Seng, Song, Sou.
Southeast Asian (Iu Mien): from the name of the Lee clan of the Iu Mien people from Laos + the Thai prefix sae, denoting a clan. Compare Lee 8.

variants.name: Leigh
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places (in at least sixteen counties, but especially Leigh in Lancashire) named either with the nominative case of Old English lēah ‘woodland clearing’ (see Lee ) or with lēage, a late dative form of this word (see Lye ).
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

en.lifeSummary: William de Lee was born in 1275, in Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, England. He had at least 1 son with Isabell Courtenay. He died in 1353, in Oxfordshire, England, at the age of 78.

fr.lifeSummary: William de Lee est né en 1275, à Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, Angleterre. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Isabell Courtenay. Il est décédé en 1353, à Oxfordshire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 78 ans.

de.lifeSummary: William de Lee wurde 1275 in Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, England geboren. Er hatte mit Isabell Courtenay mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1353 im Alter von 78 Jahren in Oxfordshire, England.

it.lifeSummary: William de Lee è nato il nel 1275 nel Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, England. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Isabell Courtenay. Morì nel 1353, nel Oxfordshire, England, all'età di 78 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: William de Leeは1275年にイギリスオクスフォードシュアイアスワルクリフで生まれた。彼はIsabell Courtenayとの間に少なくとも1人の息子をもうけた。彼は1353年にイギリスオクスフォードシュアイアで78歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: William de Lee(은)는 1275년 영국 스퍼드실르 스월클리프에서 태어났다. 그는 Isabell Courtenay(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1353년 영국 스퍼드실르에서 78세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: William de Lee nasceu em 1275, em Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, Inglaterra. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filho com Isabell Courtenay. Ele faleceu em 1353, em Oxfordshire, Inglaterra, com 78 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: William de Lee родился в 1275 г. в местности свалклифф, оксфордшир, Англия. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын от Isabell Courtenay. Он умер в 1353 г. в местности оксфордшир, Англия в возрасте 78 лет.

es.lifeSummary: William de Lee nació en 1275, en Swalcliffe, Oxfordshire, Inglaterra. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo con Isabell Courtenay. Murió en 1353, en Oxfordshire, Inglaterra, a la edad de 78 años.
